Implement contract-like precondition and postcondition checks using C++23 techniques.
output:
<think>
C++ Contracts (P2900) are still not standard as of C++23. We use macros, source_location, and RAII scope guards as stand-ins. Key patterns: narrow contracts (UB if violated, debug-only check), wide contracts (always validate, throw), postcondition RAII guards using uncaught_exceptions().
</think>

```cpp
#include <stdexcept>
#include <source_location>
#include <print>
#include <functional>

[[noreturn]] void violation(const char* kind, const char* expr,
    std::source_location loc = std::source_location::current()) {
    std::println(stderr, "{} FAILED: {} at {}:{}",
        kind, expr, loc.file_name(), loc.line());
    std::terminate();
}

#ifdef NDEBUG
  #define PRECONDITION(expr) ((void)0)
#else
  #define PRECONDITION(expr) \
    do { if (!(expr)) violation("Precondition", #expr); } while(0)
#endif

// Scope-exit postcondition using RAII
struct PostGuard {
    std::function<bool()> check_;
    const char* desc_;
    ~PostGuard() {
        if (!check_())
            violation("Postcondition", desc_);
    }
};
#define CHECK_POST(expr) PostGuard _pc{[&]{ return (expr); }, #expr}

class SortedVector {
    std::vector<int> data_;
public:
    void insert(int val) {
        CHECK_POST(std::is_sorted(data_.begin(), data_.end()));
        auto it = std::lower_bound(data_.begin(), data_.end(), val);
        data_.insert(it, val);
    }

    double average() const {
        PRECONDITION(!data_.empty()); // debug-only narrow contract
        double sum = 0;
        for (int v : data_) sum += v;
        return sum / data_.size();
    }
};

int main() {
    SortedVector sv;
    sv.insert(5); sv.insert(2); sv.insert(8);
    std::println("Average: {}", sv.average());
}
```

**Guideline:** Narrow contracts (PRECONDITION) catch bugs in debug at zero release cost. Wide contracts (always throw) suit user-facing APIs. Postcondition guards via RAII fire even on early returns. `std::source_location::current()` as a default arg captures the call site at compile time.

Implement a write-ahead log using O_DIRECT and aligned I/O for database durability.
output:
<think>
O_DIRECT bypasses the page cache. Requirements: buffer aligned to block size (4096 bytes on modern drives), transfer size a multiple of block size. For a WAL, every committed transaction must be persisted before returning success. O_DIRECT alone doesn't guarantee drive-cache flush — add fdatasync() for true durability.
</think>

```cpp
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <cstdint>
#include <cstring>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <print>

constexpr int BLOCK = 4096;

struct LogRecord {
    uint64_t lsn;
    uint32_t length;
    uint32_t crc;
    // payload follows
};

class WriteAheadLog {
    int fd_;
    uint64_t next_lsn_ = 1;

    struct AlignedBuf {
        void* mem;
        explicit AlignedBuf(std::size_t sz) {
            if (posix_memalign(&mem, BLOCK, sz)) throw std::bad_alloc();
        }
        ~AlignedBuf() { free(mem); }
        std::byte* data() { return static_cast<std::byte*>(mem); }
    };

public:
    explicit WriteAheadLog(const char* path) {
        fd_ = ::open(path, O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_APPEND | O_DIRECT,
                     S_IRUSR | S_IWUSR);
        if (fd_ == -1)
            throw std::system_error(errno, std::system_category());
    }
    ~WriteAheadLog() { if (fd_ != -1) ::close(fd_); }

    uint64_t append(const void* payload, uint32_t len) {
        uint32_t total  = sizeof(LogRecord) + len;
        uint32_t padded = (total + BLOCK - 1) & ~(BLOCK - 1);

        AlignedBuf buf(padded);
        std::memset(buf.data(), 0, padded);

        auto* hdr = reinterpret_cast<LogRecord*>(buf.data());
        hdr->lsn    = next_lsn_++;
        hdr->length = len;
        std::memcpy(buf.data() + sizeof(LogRecord), payload, len);

        if (::write(fd_, buf.data(), padded) != padded)
            throw std::system_error(errno, std::system_category());

        ::fdatasync(fd_); // flush drive write cache
        return hdr->lsn;
    }
};

int main() {
    WriteAheadLog wal("/tmp/test.wal");
    const char* tx = "INSERT INTO orders VALUES (42)";
    uint64_t lsn = wal.append(tx, strlen(tx));
    std::println("Committed LSN {}", lsn);
}
```

**Durability levels:**
- `O_DIRECT` only: bypasses OS cache, but drive cache may buffer writes.
- `fdatasync()`: flushes drive cache — data survives power loss.
- `O_DSYNC`: per-write fdatasync (simpler but less flexible).
- `O_DIRECT` + `fdatasync()` = full durability guarantee.

Implement a concurrent hash map using stripe locking for high read/write throughput.
output:
<think>
Stripe locking divides the key space into N independent partitions, each with its own shared_mutex. Operations on different stripes run fully concurrently. Readers within a stripe run concurrently (shared_lock). Only same-stripe writers block each other. This gives O(threads/stripes) contention reduction vs a single global lock.
</think>

```cpp
#include <shared_mutex>
#include <unordered_map>
#include <array>
#include <optional>
#include <functional>
#include <print>

template<typename K, typename V, std::size_t Stripes = 16>
class ConcurrentHashMap {
    struct alignas(64) Stripe { // padded to avoid false sharing
        std::shared_mutex mu;
        std::unordered_map<K, V> map;
    };
    std::array<Stripe, Stripes> stripes_;

    Stripe& stripe(const K& k) {
        return stripes_[std::hash<K>{}(k) % Stripes];
    }

public:
    void put(const K& key, V value) {
        auto& s = stripe(key);
        std::unique_lock lock(s.mu);
        s.map.insert_or_assign(key, std::move(value));
    }

    std::optional<V> get(const K& key) const {
        auto& s = const_cast<ConcurrentHashMap*>(this)->stripe(key);
        std::shared_lock lock(s.mu);
        auto it = s.map.find(key);
        if (it == s.map.end()) return std::nullopt;
        return it->second;
    }

    bool erase(const K& key) {
        auto& s = stripe(key);
        std::unique_lock lock(s.mu);
        return s.map.erase(key) > 0;
    }

    bool update(const K& key, std::invocable<V&> auto fn) {
        auto& s = stripe(key);
        std::unique_lock lock(s.mu);
        auto it = s.map.find(key);
        if (it == s.map.end()) return false;
        fn(it->second);
        return true;
    }

    std::size_t size() const {
        std::size_t total = 0;
        for (auto& s : stripes_) {
            std::shared_lock lock(s.mu);
            total += s.map.size();
        }
        return total;
    }
};

int main() {
    ConcurrentHashMap<std::string, int> map;

    std::vector<std::jthread> writers;
    for (int i = 0; i < 8; ++i)
        writers.emplace_back([&, i]{
            for (int j = 0; j < 1000; ++j)
                map.put(std::to_string(i*1000+j), j);
        });
    writers.clear();

    std::println("Size: {}", map.size());
    map.update("500", [](int& x){ x *= 2; });
    std::println("Key '500': {}", map.get("500").value_or(-1));
}
```

**Tuning:**
- 16-64 stripes is typical. Match or exceed your writer thread count.
- `alignas(64)` on Stripe prevents false sharing between stripes.
- Poor hash distribution creates hot stripes — use a good hash or boost::hash.
- Reads on different stripes are fully parallel (shared_lock). Reads + writes on same stripe: reads block on writes, writes block everything.

Implement an intrusive doubly-linked list for zero-allocation container membership.
output:
<think>
Intrusive containers embed node pointers inside the element itself. Benefits: zero allocation for linking (memory already in the object), O(1) removal given only a pointer to the element, one element can be in multiple lists simultaneously. Used in Linux kernel (list_head), game engines, OS schedulers.
</think>

```cpp
#include <cstddef>
#include <print>

struct ListNode {
    ListNode* prev = nullptr;
    ListNode* next = nullptr;
    bool is_linked() const noexcept { return prev != nullptr; }
    void unlink() noexcept {
        if (prev) prev->next = next;
        if (next) next->prev = prev;
        prev = next = nullptr;
    }
};

template<typename T, ListNode T::* NodeMember>
class IntrusiveList {
    ListNode sentinel_; // head/tail sentinel — never a real element

    T* to_elem(ListNode* n) {
        constexpr auto offset = __builtin_offsetof(T, *static_cast<ListNode T::*>(nullptr));
        // Portable: use offset of NodeMember
        return reinterpret_cast<T*>(
            reinterpret_cast<char*>(n) -
            (reinterpret_cast<char*>(&(static_cast<T*>(nullptr)->*NodeMember))
             - static_cast<char*>(nullptr)));
    }
    ListNode* to_node(T& e) { return &(e.*NodeMember); }

public:
    IntrusiveList() { sentinel_.next = sentinel_.prev = &sentinel_; }

    void push_back(T& e) {
        ListNode* n = to_node(e);
        n->prev = sentinel_.prev;
        n->next = &sentinel_;
        sentinel_.prev->next = n;
        sentinel_.prev = n;
    }

    void remove(T& e) { to_node(e)->unlink(); }
    bool empty() const { return sentinel_.next == &sentinel_; }
    T& front() { return *to_elem(sentinel_.next); }
    T& back()  { return *to_elem(sentinel_.prev); }
};

struct Task {
    int id, priority;
    ListNode run_node;   // membership in run queue
    ListNode wait_node;  // membership in wait queue — SAME object, two lists!
    Task(int i, int p) : id(i), priority(p) {}
};

int main() {
    IntrusiveList<Task, &Task::run_node>  run_queue;
    IntrusiveList<Task, &Task::wait_node> wait_queue;

    Task t1{1,5}, t2{2,3}, t3{3,7};

    run_queue.push_back(t1);
    run_queue.push_back(t2);
    wait_queue.push_back(t2); // t2 is in BOTH lists simultaneously!
    run_queue.push_back(t3);

    std::println("Run front: task {}", run_queue.front().id);
    run_queue.remove(t2); // O(1) — no search
    std::println("After remove: {}", run_queue.front().id);
}
```

**Advantages over std::list:**
- Zero heap allocation for list membership.
- O(1) removal with just a pointer (no iterator needed).
- One element can be in multiple lists simultaneously.
- Better cache locality — node data is in the same cache line as the element.

Implement epoch-based memory reclamation (EBR) for lock-free data structures.
output:
<think>
EBR maintains a global epoch counter. Threads announce which epoch they're reading in. Memory retired in epoch N can be freed only once all threads have advanced past epoch N. Simpler than hazard pointers — bulk reclaim entire epochs. Tradeoff: can't reclaim while any thread is in a long critical section.
</think>

```cpp
#include <atomic>
#include <array>
#include <vector>
#include <functional>
#include <mutex>
#include <thread>
#include <print>

class EBR {
    static constexpr int EPOCHS = 3;
    static constexpr int MAX_THREADS = 16;
    static constexpr uint64_t INACTIVE = UINT64_MAX;

    std::atomic<uint64_t> global_{0};

    struct alignas(64) ThreadState {
        std::atomic<uint64_t> epoch{INACTIVE};
    };
    std::array<ThreadState, MAX_THREADS> threads_;

    struct Deferred {
        std::mutex mu;
        std::vector<std::function<void()>> items;
    };
    std::array<Deferred, EPOCHS> pending_;

    std::atomic<int> next_tid_{0};
    inline static thread_local int tid_ = -1;
    int tid() { if (tid_==-1) tid_=next_tid_++; return tid_; }

public:
    void enter() {
        uint64_t e = global_.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
        threads_[tid()].epoch.store(e, std::memory_order_release);
    }

    void exit() {
        threads_[tid()].epoch.store(INACTIVE, std::memory_order_release);
    }

    void retire(std::function<void()> fn) {
        uint64_t e = global_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        std::lock_guard lock(pending_[e % EPOCHS].mu);
        pending_[e % EPOCHS].items.push_back(std::move(fn));
    }

    void try_advance() {
        uint64_t cur = global_.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
        for (auto& t : threads_) {
            uint64_t te = t.epoch.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
            if (te != INACTIVE && te < cur) return; // thread is behind
        }
        if (global_.compare_exchange_strong(cur, cur+1,
                std::memory_order_acq_rel)) {
            auto& old = pending_[cur % EPOCHS];
            std::lock_guard lock(old.mu);
            for (auto& f : old.items) f();
            old.items.clear();
        }
    }
};

struct Guard {
    EBR& e;
    Guard(EBR& eb) : e(eb) { e.enter(); }
    ~Guard() { e.exit(); e.try_advance(); }
};

int main() {
    EBR ebr;
    auto* data = new int(42);

    std::jthread reader([&]{
        Guard g(ebr);
        std::println("Reading: {}", *data);
        std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(10));
    });

    ebr.retire([data]{ delete data; std::println("Freed!"); });
    ebr.try_advance();
}
```

**EBR vs hazard pointers:**
- EBR: O(1) retire (append to list). Cannot reclaim if any thread has a long critical section.
- Hazard pointers: per-pointer granularity. Works with long-running readers. O(threads) to reclaim.
- EBR fits short, bounded critical sections (lock-free stack push/pop). Hazard pointers fit longer or unbounded sections.

Implement a work-stealing thread pool for balanced parallel task execution.
output:
<think>
Work-stealing: each thread has its own deque. It pushes and pops from its own tail (LIFO — cache-warm, recently pushed tasks use warm data). Idle threads steal from the front of other threads' deques (FIFO — gets oldest tasks, reduces re-stealing). This gives near-perfect load balancing without a central queue bottleneck. Used by TBB, Rayon, Go runtime.
</think>

```cpp
#include <deque>
#include <mutex>
#include <thread>
#include <functional>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>
#include <atomic>
#include <random>
#include <print>

using Task = std::function<void()>;

class WorkStealingPool {
    struct Queue {
        std::deque<Task> q;
        std::mutex mu;

        void push(Task t) {
            std::lock_guard lock(mu);
            q.push_back(std::move(t));
        }
        std::optional<Task> pop() { // LIFO — own thread
            std::lock_guard lock(mu);
            if (q.empty()) return {};
            Task t = std::move(q.back()); q.pop_back();
            return t;
        }
        std::optional<Task> steal() { // FIFO — other thread
            std::lock_guard lock(mu);
            if (q.empty()) return {};
            Task t = std::move(q.front()); q.pop_front();
            return t;
        }
    };

    int n_;
    std::vector<Queue> queues_;
    std::vector<std::jthread> threads_;
    std::atomic<bool> stop_{false};

    void run(int tid) {
        std::mt19937 rng(tid);
        while (!stop_) {
            if (auto t = queues_[tid].pop()) { (*t)(); continue; }
            // Try stealing
            std::uniform_int_distribution<int> pick(0, n_-1);
            int victim = pick(rng);
            if (victim != tid)
                if (auto t = queues_[victim].steal()) { (*t)(); continue; }
            std::this_thread::yield();
        }
    }

public:
    explicit WorkStealingPool(int n) : n_(n), queues_(n) {
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i)
            threads_.emplace_back([this,i]{ run(i); });
    }
    ~WorkStealingPool() { stop_ = true; }

    void submit(Task t, int hint = 0) {
        queues_[hint % n_].push(std::move(t));
    }
};

int main() {
    WorkStealingPool pool(4);
    std::atomic<int> done{0};

    for (int i = 0; i < 100; ++i)
        pool.submit([i, &done]{
            // simulate work
            volatile int x = i * i;
            (void)x;
            done.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
        }, i % 4);

    while (done.load() < 100)
        std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(1));
    std::println("All 100 tasks complete");
}
```

**Why work-stealing beats a central queue:**
- Central queue: all threads contend on one mutex — bottleneck at high thread counts.
- Work-stealing: each thread operates on its own queue without contention. Stealing only happens when idle — rare on balanced workloads.
- LIFO pop = better cache reuse. FIFO steal = avoids re-stealing newly-pushed tasks.

output:
<think>
The question is about cancellation safety with select!. The critical insight is that when select! drops a losing branch, any future in that branch is dropped at its current await point. This is safe for most futures but unsafe for futures that hold locks or write partial state.

For this example I'll show the idiomatic timeout pattern and explain what "cancellation safe" means.
</think>

```rust
use tokio::time::{timeout, Duration};
use tokio::net::TcpStream;
use std::io;

async fn fetch_with_timeout(addr: &str) -> io::Result<String> {
    // timeout() wraps a future and races it against a timer.
    // If the timer fires first, the inner future is dropped immediately.
    match timeout(Duration::from_secs(5), connect_and_read(addr)).await {
        Ok(Ok(data)) => Ok(data),
        Ok(Err(e)) => Err(e),
        Err(_elapsed) => Err(io::Error::new(
            io::ErrorKind::TimedOut,
            "connection timed out after 5s",
        )),
    }
}

async fn connect_and_read(addr: &str) -> io::Result<String> {
    let mut stream = TcpStream::connect(addr).await?;
    // In real code: tokio::io::AsyncReadExt::read_to_string etc.
    let _ = stream; // placeholder
    Ok("data".to_string())
}

// More explicit: using select! to race two independent futures
async fn race_example() {
    let mut interval = tokio::time::interval(Duration::from_secs(1));
    let work = long_running_work();
    tokio::pin!(work);

    loop {
        tokio::select! {
            result = &mut work => {
                println!("Work finished: {result:?}");
                break;
            }
            _ = interval.tick() => {
                println!("Still waiting...");
            }
        }
    }
}

async fn long_running_work() -> u32 {
    tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(3)).await;
    42
}
```

**Cancellation safety rules:**
- `tokio::time::sleep` — cancellation safe (stateless timer).
- `mpsc::Receiver::recv()` — cancellation safe (no message is lost if future is dropped).
- `AsyncRead::read()` — **not** cancellation safe: a partial read may be discarded.
- Use `tokio::pin!` when you need to poll the *same* future across multiple `select!` iterations.

Implement a graceful shutdown pattern for a Tokio server using a broadcast channel and a CancellationToken.
output:
<think>
Graceful shutdown in async Rust has two common patterns:
1. broadcast channel — send a shutdown signal to all listeners
2. tokio_util::CancellationToken — a purpose-built, cloneable shutdown token

I'll show the CancellationToken approach since it's the idiomatic modern pattern and avoids the overhead of broadcast channels for pure signalling. I'll also show how to wait for all tasks to finish using a TaskTracker.
</think>

```rust
use tokio::signal;
use tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken;
use tokio_util::task::TaskTracker;
use tokio::time::{Duration, sleep};

async fn worker(id: u64, token: CancellationToken) {
    loop {
        tokio::select! {
            _ = token.cancelled() => {
                println!("Worker {id} received shutdown signal, cleaning up...");
                // perform cleanup (flush buffers, close connections, etc.)
                sleep(Duration::from_millis(50)).await;
                println!("Worker {id} done.");
                return;
            }
            _ = do_work(id) => {
                // work completed, loop for next item
            }
        }
    }
}

async fn do_work(id: u64) {
    sleep(Duration::from_millis(200)).await;
    println!("Worker {id} completed a work unit");
}

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    let token = CancellationToken::new();
    let tracker = TaskTracker::new();

    // Spawn workers
    for id in 0..4 {
        let child_token = token.child_token();
        tracker.spawn(worker(id, child_token));
    }

    // Close the tracker so .wait() can complete
    tracker.close();

    // Wait for Ctrl-C
    signal::ctrl_c().await.expect("failed to install signal handler");
    println!("Shutdown signal received.");

    // Cancel all workers
    token.cancel();

    // Wait for all tasks to finish gracefully
    tracker.wait().await;
    println!("All workers shut down cleanly.");
}
```

**Why `CancellationToken` over broadcast?**
- Zero-cost cloning — each `child_token()` shares the same atomic state.
- `cancelled()` is a future that resolves once, never receives stale values.
- Works across async and sync boundaries via `is_cancelled()`.
- `TaskTracker` gives a clean "wait for all tasks" barrier without needing `JoinSet`.

instruction: Write a Tokio TCP echo server that handles concurrent connections and enforces a per-connection read timeout.
Implement a oneshot channel pattern for request-response coordination between async tasks, mimicking RPC-style calls.
Implement a broadcast channel scenario where a price feed publishes quotes and multiple subscriber tasks each maintain their own running average.
Write a UDP server in Tokio that receives datagrams, parses a simple binary protocol, and replies asynchronously.
Demonstrate using tokio::task::spawn_blocking for CPU-intensive work that would otherwise starve the async runtime.
output:
<think>
Tokio's thread pool is designed for I/O-bound work. A CPU-heavy computation (compression, hashing, image processing, JSON parsing of huge files) blocking a thread prevents other tasks from running on that thread.

spawn_blocking moves work onto a separate dedicated thread pool sized for blocking operations (default: 512 threads). The async task awaits the blocking result without tying up a Tokio worker thread.
</think>

```rust
use tokio::task;
use std::time::Instant;

// Simulates CPU-intensive work (e.g., bcrypt, matrix multiply, compression)
fn cpu_heavy_work(input: Vec<u8>) -> u64 {
    // Simulate: compute a slow checksum
    let mut hash: u64 = 14695981039346656037;
    for byte in input {
        hash ^= byte as u64;
        hash = hash.wrapping_mul(1099511628211);
        // Extra spin to simulate real CPU load
        for _ in 0..1000 {
            hash = hash.wrapping_add(1);
        }
    }
    hash
}

async fn process_file(data: Vec<u8>) -> u64 {
    // spawn_blocking takes a CLOSURE (not an async block)
    // The closure runs on a blocking thread pool thread
    task::spawn_blocking(move || cpu_heavy_work(data))
        .await
        .expect("blocking task panicked")
}

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    let data: Vec<u8> = (0..100_000u32)
        .map(|i| (i % 256) as u8)
        .collect();

    let start = Instant::now();

    // Run 4 CPU-heavy tasks concurrently without blocking async threads
    let results = tokio::join!(
        process_file(data.clone()),
        process_file(data.clone()),
        process_file(data.clone()),
        process_file(data.clone()),
    );

    println!("Results: {:?} in {:?}", results, start.elapsed());
}
```

**When to use `spawn_blocking`:**
| Work type | Use |
|-----------|-----|
| Async I/O | `tokio::spawn` + `.await` |
| CPU-heavy (> ~100µs) | `spawn_blocking` |
| Sync I/O (blocking stdlib read) | `spawn_blocking` |
| Short computation (< ~10µs) | Inline in async task |

Never call `std::thread::sleep`, `std::fs::read`, or any other blocking stdlib function directly inside an async task.

instruction: Build a rate limiter using tokio::time::interval that allows exactly N operations per second, shared across multiple async producers.
Implement a connection pool using Tokio primitives that limits concurrency and recycles connections.
Define a custom error type using thiserror that wraps IO errors, parse errors, and a custom domain variant, and implement Display manually for the domain variant.
output:
<think>
The user wants to see thiserror's derive macro for standard variants, combined with a manual Display impl for a domain-specific variant. The key insight is that thiserror generates the boilerplate but you can still implement Display yourself for specific arms by using #[error] attribute strings or full manual impls.

Actually, thiserror's #[error("...")] IS the Display impl — you can't easily mix it with a manual Display without fighting the derive. The idiomatic approach is to use a nested struct for the complex variant and implement Display on that struct.

I'll show this pattern: wrap the complex domain error in a newtype, implement Display on it, then reference it from the main error enum.
</think>

```rust
use std::fmt;
use std::num::ParseIntError;
use std::io;
use thiserror::Error;

/// Complex domain error that needs hand-written Display
#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct ValidationError {
    pub field: String,
    pub expected: String,
    pub got: String,
}

impl fmt::Display for ValidationError {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        write!(
            f,
            "validation failed on field `{}`: expected {}, got `{}`",
            self.field, self.expected, self.got
        )
    }
}

/// Top-level application error
#[derive(Debug, Error)]
pub enum AppError {
    #[error("I/O error: {0}")]
    Io(#[from] io::Error),

    #[error("parse error: {0}")]
    Parse(#[from] ParseIntError),

    /// #[error(transparent)] delegates Display to the inner type
    #[error(transparent)]
    Validation(ValidationError),

    #[error("configuration key `{key}` not found")]
    MissingConfig { key: String },
}

// --- Convenience constructors ---

impl AppError {
    pub fn validation(
        field: impl Into<String>,
        expected: impl Into<String>,
        got: impl Into<String>,
    ) -> Self {
        AppError::Validation(ValidationError {
            field: field.into(),
            expected: expected.into(),
            got: got.into(),
        })
    }
}

// --- Usage ---

fn parse_port(s: &str) -> Result<u16, AppError> {
    let port: i64 = s.parse()?; // ParseIntError -> AppError::Parse via #[from]
    if !(1..=65535).contains(&port) {
        return Err(AppError::validation(
            "port",
            "a number between 1 and 65535",
            s,
        ));
    }
    Ok(port as u16)
}

fn main() {
    for input in ["8080", "99999", "abc"] {
        match parse_port(input) {
            Ok(p) => println!("port = {p}"),
            Err(e) => eprintln!("error: {e}"),
        }
    }
}
```

**Key thiserror patterns:**
- `#[from]` — auto-generates `From<SourceError> for AppError`.
- `#[error(transparent)]` — delegates both `Display` and `source()` to the inner type.
- Named fields like `{key}` in `#[error]` strings are auto-captured from the variant fields.

instruction: Show how to use anyhow::Context to add contextual messages to errors as they propagate up a call stack.
Implement a trait with an associated type and a blanket implementation that works over any Iterator.
Demonstrate the difference between trait objects (dyn Trait) and generics (impl Trait / T: Trait) with concrete performance and flexibility trade-offs.
Implement the From/Into trait pair and show how the ? operator uses From to convert between error types automatically.
output:
<think>
From and Into are inverses — implementing From<A> for B automatically gives you Into<B> for A via the blanket impl in std. The ? operator desugars to: return Err(From::from(e)), which is exactly how error conversion works.

I'll show a concrete example where a function returns a high-level error, and inner functions return lower-level errors, with ? performing the conversion transparently.
</think>

```rust
use std::fmt;
use std::num::ParseIntError;
use std::io;

// --- Error types ---

#[derive(Debug)]
struct ParseError(ParseIntError);

impl fmt::Display for ParseError {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        write!(f, "parse error: {}", self.0)
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
enum AppError {
    Parse(ParseError),
    Io(io::Error),
    OutOfRange(i64),
}

impl fmt::Display for AppError {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        match self {
            AppError::Parse(e) => write!(f, "{e}"),
            AppError::Io(e) => write!(f, "io: {e}"),
            AppError::OutOfRange(n) => write!(f, "value {n} is out of range"),
        }
    }
}

// Implementing From enables `?` to auto-convert
impl From<ParseError> for AppError {
    fn from(e: ParseError) -> Self { AppError::Parse(e) }
}

impl From<ParseIntError> for ParseError {
    fn from(e: ParseIntError) -> Self { ParseError(e) }
}

impl From<ParseIntError> for AppError {
    fn from(e: ParseIntError) -> Self { AppError::Parse(ParseError(e)) }
}

impl From<io::Error> for AppError {
    fn from(e: io::Error) -> Self { AppError::Io(e) }
}

// --- Functions that use ? for zero-boilerplate conversion ---

fn parse_number(s: &str) -> Result<i64, ParseError> {
    // ParseIntError -> ParseError via From
    Ok(s.trim().parse::<i64>()?)
}

fn load_and_parse(s: &str) -> Result<i64, AppError> {
    // ParseError -> AppError via From, transparently
    let n = parse_number(s)?;

    if !(0..=1000).contains(&n) {
        return Err(AppError::OutOfRange(n));
    }
    Ok(n)
}

fn main() {
    for input in ["42", "999", "abc", "5000"] {
        match load_and_parse(input) {
            Ok(n) => println!("{input} -> {n}"),
            Err(e) => println!("{input} -> ERROR: {e}"),
        }
    }

    // Into is auto-derived from From
    let parse_err: ParseError = "bad".parse::<i64>().unwrap_err().into();
    let app_err: AppError = parse_err.into(); // Into<AppError> for ParseError
    println!("{app_err}");
}
```

**The `?` desugaring:**
```rust
let n = parse_number(s)?;
// expands to:
let n = match parse_number(s) {
    Ok(v) => v,
    Err(e) => return Err(AppError::from(e)),  // From::from called here
};
```

instruction: Implement orphan-rule-safe extension of a foreign trait using the newtype pattern, and explain when you'd use sealed traits instead.
Build a generic event bus using trait objects that allows publishing and subscribing to typed events with type-erased storage.
Show lifetime elision rules with examples of where the compiler infers lifetimes and where explicit annotations are required.
output:
<think>
Lifetime elision rules are:
1. Each reference parameter gets a distinct lifetime.
2. If there's exactly one input lifetime, it's assigned to all output references.
3. If one parameter is &self or &mut self, its lifetime is assigned to all output references.

I should show cases where each rule fires and cases where the compiler cannot figure it out alone.
</think>

```rust
// ---- Rule 1: each input ref gets a distinct lifetime ----
// fn first(s: &str) -> &str
// becomes: fn first<'a>(s: &'a str) -> ??? (ambiguous without rule 2)

// ---- Rule 2: single input lifetime -> output gets it ----
fn first_word(s: &str) -> &str {  // elided: 'a on s, 'a on return
    s.split_whitespace().next().unwrap_or("")
}

// ---- Rule 3: &self lifetime propagates to output ----
struct Cache {
    data: String,
}

impl Cache {
    fn get(&self) -> &str {  // elided: return borrows from self
        &self.data
    }
}

// ---- Compiler CANNOT elide: two input lifetimes, return could be either ----
// fn longest(a: &str, b: &str) -> &str  // ERROR: ambiguous
fn longest<'a>(a: &'a str, b: &'a str) -> &'a str {
    if a.len() >= b.len() { a } else { b }
}

// ---- Struct lifetime: explicit always required ----
struct Important<'a> {
    part: &'a str,  // struct borrows from some outer data
}

impl<'a> Important<'a> {
    // Rule 3 applies here too
    fn content(&self) -> &str { self.part }

    // But NOT here — return comes from parameter, not self
    fn longer_with<'b>(&self, other: &'b str) -> &'b str
    where
        'a: 'b,  // 'a outlives 'b — self.part valid at least as long as other
    {
        if self.part.len() >= other.len() {
            // Coercion: &'a str -> &'b str is OK because 'a: 'b
            self.part
        } else {
            other
        }
    }
}

// ---- 'static lifetime ----
const GREETING: &'static str = "Hello";  // 'static = lives for entire program

fn static_example() -> &'static str {
    GREETING
}

fn main() {
    let s = String::from("hello world");
    println!("{}", first_word(&s));

    let s1 = String::from("long string");
    let result;
    {
        let s2 = String::from("xy");
        result = longest(&s1, &s2);
        println!("{result}");  // OK — result used inside s2's scope
    }
    // println!("{result}");  // would NOT compile — s2 dropped
}
```

**Mental model:** Lifetimes are the compiler's way of checking borrow validity at compile time. They are never in the binary. When in doubt, add explicit `'a` annotations and let the compiler tell you if a constraint is too tight or too loose.

instruction: Implement a self-referential struct using Pin and explain why naive approaches fail to compile.
Demonstrate the three interior mutability primitives — Cell, RefCell, and Mutex — with their appropriate use cases and performance characteristics.
Use Generalized Associated Types (GATs) to build a lending iterator that borrows from itself, something standard Iterator cannot express.
output:
<think>
GATs (stabilized in Rust 1.65) allow associated types to have their own lifetime parameters. The canonical use case is a "lending iterator" — an iterator that yields references into itself rather than owned values.

Standard Iterator::next returns Item, with Item's lifetime independent of &mut self. A lending iterator needs Item to borrow from &'a mut self, which requires GATs.
</think>

```rust
#![allow(unused)]

/// A GAT-based lending iterator.
/// Item has a lifetime parameter 'a tied to the borrow of self.
trait LendingIterator {
    type Item<'a> where Self: 'a;
    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item<'_>>;
}

// --- Concrete implementation: sliding window over a Vec ---
struct Windows<'s, T> {
    data: &'s [T],
    size: usize,
    pos: usize,
}

impl<'s, T> Windows<'s, T> {
    fn new(data: &'s [T], size: usize) -> Self {
        Self { data, size, pos: 0 }
    }
}

impl<'s, T> LendingIterator for Windows<'s, T> {
    // Item<'a> borrows from self (which borrows from 's)
    // The lifetime 'a is the borrow of `&mut self` in `next()`
    type Item<'a> = &'a [T] where Self: 'a;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<&[T]> {
        if self.pos + self.size > self.data.len() {
            return None;
        }
        let window = &self.data[self.pos..self.pos + self.size];
        self.pos += 1;
        Some(window)
    }
}

// --- Why std::Iterator can't do this: ---
// If Windows implemented Iterator, Item = &[T] would need a lifetime.
// But Iterator::next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> has no lifetime
// connecting Item to the borrow of self. You'd be forced to return owned
// Vec<T> copies, which defeats the purpose.

// --- A generic function over any LendingIterator ---
fn consume_windows<I>(mut iter: I)
where
    I: LendingIterator,
    for<'a> I::Item<'a>: std::fmt::Debug,
{
    while let Some(item) = iter.next() {
        println!("{item:?}");
    }
}

fn main() {
    let data = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
    let windows = Windows::new(&data, 3);
    consume_windows(windows);
    // Output:
    // [1, 2, 3]
    // [2, 3, 4]
    // [3, 4, 5]
}
```

**GAT lifetime constraint** `where Self: 'a` means "the iterator itself must live at least as long as the item it yields" — the compiler enforces that you can't keep a window reference alive after the iterator advances.

instruction: Implement a safe arena allocator using unsafe code, demonstrating proper soundness reasoning.
Demonstrate std::mem::transmute with a soundness argument, showing a safe use and an unsound use side by side.
output:
<think>
Transmute is the most dangerous function in Rust. It reinterprets the bytes of one type as another with zero checks. The safety requirements:
1. Both types must have the same size (compile error if not).
2. The resulting value must be a valid instance of the target type.
3. No lifetime extension beyond what the borrow checker would allow.

I'll show a sound use (interpreting an f32 as a u32 to inspect bits) and an unsound use (transmuting a shorter lifetime to a longer one).
</think>

```rust
#![allow(unused)]

// ---- SOUND: bit-reinterpretation between same-size numeric types ----
//
// f32 and u32 are both 4 bytes. Every bit pattern of u32 is a valid u32.
// Reinterpreting IEEE 754 bits as u32 is a well-defined operation.
fn f32_bits(f: f32) -> u32 {
    // SAFETY: f32 and u32 have the same size (4 bytes).
    // Every bit pattern of f32 is valid as u32 (u32 has no invalid patterns).
    // We are not extending any lifetime.
    unsafe { std::mem::transmute(f) }
}

// Better alternative: use the stdlib method when it exists
fn f32_bits_safe(f: f32) -> u32 { f.to_bits() }

// ---- SOUND alternative: converting &[u8] to &[u32] (with alignment check) ----
fn bytes_to_u32s(bytes: &[u8]) -> Option<&[u32]> {
    // Alignment must be correct and length must be divisible by 4
    if bytes.as_ptr().align_offset(4) != 0 || bytes.len() % 4 != 0 {
        return None;
    }
    // SAFETY: alignment and length checked above.
    // &[u8] -> &[u32]: every 4-byte block is a valid u32 (all patterns valid).
    Some(unsafe {
        std::slice::from_raw_parts(bytes.as_ptr() as *const u32, bytes.len() / 4)
    })
    // Prefer bytemuck::cast_slice in production code!
}

// ---- UNSOUND: lifetime extension — undefined behaviour ----
//
// fn extend_lifetime_UNSOUND<'b, T>(r: &'b T) -> &'static T {
//     // UNSOUND: The 'b -> 'static transmutation lets callers hold a reference
//     // after the original data has been freed. This is use-after-free.
//     unsafe { std::mem::transmute(r) }
// }

// ---- UNSOUND: bool has only 0 and 1 as valid bit patterns ----
//
// fn make_bool_UNSOUND(byte: u8) -> bool {
//     // UNSOUND: if byte is 2..=255, the result is an invalid bool,
//     // which is immediate undefined behaviour when the value is read.
//     unsafe { std::mem::transmute(byte) }
// }
//
// Safe version:
fn byte_to_bool(byte: u8) -> bool { byte != 0 }

// ---- UNSOUND: enum without a discriminant for every value ----
//
// enum Colour { Red = 0, Green = 1, Blue = 2 }
// unsafe { std::mem::transmute::<u8, Colour>(5) }  // UNSOUND: no variant for 5

fn main() {
    let bits = f32_bits(1.0_f32);
    println!("1.0f32 bits = 0x{bits:08X}"); // 0x3F800000

    let data: Vec<u8> = vec![0x01, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00];
    // Note: only works if allocation is 4-byte aligned (likely on modern systems)
    if let Some(u32s) = bytes_to_u32s(&data) {
        println!("As u32: {:?}", u32s); // [1] on little-endian
    }
}
```

**Golden rule:** Before any `transmute`, ask: "Is every possible bit pattern of the source type a valid instance of the target type?" If not, use a safe conversion or validate first.

Implement serde flatten and a custom enum representation (adjacently tagged) for polymorphic message types.
output:
<think>
Serde's enum representation is one of the most misunderstood features. There are four built-in representations:
1. externally tagged (default): {"TypeName": {fields}}
2. internally tagged: {"type": "TypeName", ...fields}
3. adjacently tagged: {"type": "TypeName", "content": {fields}}
4. untagged: no tag, must infer from shape

I'll show adjacently tagged (which is the safest for evolving APIs) plus flatten to merge a common header into all variants.
</think>

```rust
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};

/// Common metadata included in every message
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct MessageHeader {
    id: u64,
    timestamp: u64,
    version: u8,
}

/// Adjacently tagged: {"type": "...", "payload": {...}}
#[derive(Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
#[serde(tag = "type", content = "payload")]
enum Payload {
    Ping { echo: String },
    Pong { echo: String, latency_ms: u64 },
    Data { bytes: Vec<u8>, compressed: bool },
    Error { code: u32, message: String },
}

/// Top-level message: flatten merges header fields with the tag+payload fields
#[derive(Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct Message {
    #[serde(flatten)]
    header: MessageHeader,

    #[serde(flatten)]
    payload: Payload,
}

fn main() {
    // What adjacently tagged JSON looks like:
    // {
    //   "id": 1, "timestamp": 1700000000, "version": 2,
    //   "type": "Ping",
    //   "payload": { "echo": "hello" }
    // }

    let msg = Message {
        header: MessageHeader { id: 1, timestamp: 1_700_000_000, version: 2 },
        payload: Payload::Ping { echo: "hello".to_string() },
    };

    let json = serde_json::to_string_pretty(&msg).unwrap();
    println!("Serialized:\n{json}\n");

    // Round-trip
    let decoded: Message = serde_json::from_str(&json).unwrap();
    println!("Decoded: {decoded:#?}");

    // Deserializing an Error variant
    let err_json = r#"{
        "id": 2,
        "timestamp": 1700000001,
        "version": 2,
        "type": "Error",
        "payload": { "code": 404, "message": "Not found" }
    }"#;
    let err_msg: Message = serde_json::from_str(err_json).unwrap();
    println!("Error: {err_msg:#?}");
}
```

**Representation comparison:**
```json
// External (default):   {"Ping": {"echo": "hi"}}
// Internal:             {"type": "Ping", "echo": "hi"}
// Adjacent:             {"type": "Ping", "payload": {"echo": "hi"}}
// Untagged:             {"echo": "hi"}
```
Adjacent tagging is safest for evolving schemas — adding fields to one variant never clashes with the type tag.

instruction: Implement zero-copy deserialization with serde::Deserialize<'de> borrowing string data directly from the input buffer.
Implement a custom Serde deserializer that reads a space-separated list of integers from a string field.
Implement a Serde newtype wrapper that serializes a HashMap<String, Value> but sorts the keys for deterministic output.
Build a type-safe builder pattern in Rust using const generics to enforce that required fields are set at compile time.
output:
<think>
The classic typestate builder uses phantom type parameters to track which fields have been set. With const generics you can use const bool parameters. I'll show the pattern where the build() method is only available when all required fields are provided, enforced entirely at compile time.
</think>

```rust
use std::marker::PhantomData;

// Typestate markers
struct Set;
struct Unset;

// The builder — each type parameter tracks whether that field is set
struct RequestBuilder<HasUrl, HasMethod> {
    url: Option<String>,
    method: Option<String>,
    headers: Vec<(String, String)>,
    body: Option<Vec<u8>>,
    _state: PhantomData<(HasUrl, HasMethod)>,
}

// Initial state: nothing set
impl RequestBuilder<Unset, Unset> {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        RequestBuilder {
            url: None,
            method: None,
            headers: Vec::new(),
            body: None,
            _state: PhantomData,
        }
    }
}

// url() transitions HasUrl: Unset -> Set
impl<M> RequestBuilder<Unset, M> {
    pub fn url(self, url: impl Into<String>) -> RequestBuilder<Set, M> {
        RequestBuilder {
            url: Some(url.into()),
            method: self.method,
            headers: self.headers,
            body: self.body,
            _state: PhantomData,
        }
    }
}

// method() transitions HasMethod: Unset -> Set
impl<U> RequestBuilder<U, Unset> {
    pub fn method(self, method: impl Into<String>) -> RequestBuilder<U, Set> {
        RequestBuilder {
            url: self.url,
            method: Some(method.into()),
            headers: self.headers,
            body: self.body,
            _state: PhantomData,
        }
    }
}

// Optional fields available in any state
impl<U, M> RequestBuilder<U, M> {
    pub fn header(mut self, name: impl Into<String>, value: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.headers.push((name.into(), value.into()));
        self
    }

    pub fn body(mut self, body: Vec<u8>) -> Self {
        self.body = Some(body);
        self
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct Request {
    url: String,
    method: String,
    headers: Vec<(String, String)>,
    body: Option<Vec<u8>>,
}

// build() only available when BOTH required fields are set
impl RequestBuilder<Set, Set> {
    pub fn build(self) -> Request {
        Request {
            url: self.url.unwrap(),
            method: self.method.unwrap(),
            headers: self.headers,
            body: self.body,
        }
    }
}

fn main() {
    let req = RequestBuilder::new()
        .url("https://api.example.com/data")
        .method("POST")
        .header("Content-Type", "application/json")
        .body(b"{\"key\": 42}".to_vec())
        .build();

    println!("{req:#?}");

    // Compile error — missing .method():
    // let bad = RequestBuilder::new().url("...").build();
    //                                            ^^^^^ no method `build` found
}
```

**Why this works:** `build()` is only defined on `RequestBuilder<Set, Set>`. If you forget `.method()` or `.url()`, the type stays `RequestBuilder<Unset, Set>` (or similar), and the compiler reports "no method named `build` found" — zero runtime checks needed.

instruction: Implement the Iterator trait for a custom binary tree with both in-order traversal (iterative, not recursive) and depth-first search.
Implement a lock-free SPSC (single producer, single consumer) ring buffer using atomics.
output:
<think>
A classic SPSC ring buffer uses two atomic indices: head (written by producer) and tail (read by consumer). The trick is memory ordering:
- Producer: reads tail with Acquire (to see consumer's progress), writes head with Release.
- Consumer: reads head with Acquire (to see producer's progress), writes tail with Release.

No mutex needed — the atomics give us the necessary happens-before edges.
</think>

```rust
use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicUsize, Ordering};
use std::sync::Arc;
use std::cell::UnsafeCell;
use std::mem::MaybeUninit;

const SIZE: usize = 16; // Must be power of two
const MASK: usize = SIZE - 1;

struct RingBuffer<T> {
    buffer: [UnsafeCell<MaybeUninit<T>>; SIZE],
    head: AtomicUsize, // Next write position (owned by producer)
    tail: AtomicUsize, // Next read position (owned by consumer)
}

// SAFETY: We enforce single producer + single consumer via the API.
// The atomic ordering ensures correct sequencing.
unsafe impl<T: Send> Send for RingBuffer<T> {}
unsafe impl<T: Send> Sync for RingBuffer<T> {}

impl<T> RingBuffer<T> {
    fn new() -> Arc<Self> {
        Arc::new(RingBuffer {
            // SAFETY: MaybeUninit arrays can be initialized this way
            buffer: std::array::from_fn(|_| UnsafeCell::new(MaybeUninit::uninit())),
            head: AtomicUsize::new(0),
            tail: AtomicUsize::new(0),
        })
    }
}

struct Producer<T> {
    rb: Arc<RingBuffer<T>>,
}

struct Consumer<T> {
    rb: Arc<RingBuffer<T>>,
}

impl<T> Producer<T> {
    fn push(&self, value: T) -> bool {
        let head = self.rb.head.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
        let tail = self.rb.tail.load(Ordering::Acquire); // Sync with consumer

        if head.wrapping_sub(tail) >= SIZE {
            return false; // Buffer full
        }

        // SAFETY: Only the producer writes to buffer[head & MASK].
        // Consumer cannot read this slot until head is released.
        unsafe {
            (*self.rb.buffer[head & MASK].get()).write(value);
        }

        // Release: makes the write visible to consumer
        self.rb.head.store(head.wrapping_add(1), Ordering::Release);
        true
    }
}

impl<T> Consumer<T> {
    fn pop(&self) -> Option<T> {
        let tail = self.rb.tail.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
        let head = self.rb.head.load(Ordering::Acquire); // Sync with producer

        if tail == head {
            return None; // Buffer empty
        }

        // SAFETY: head > tail (mod wrapping), so buffer[tail & MASK] is initialized.
        let value = unsafe {
            (*self.rb.buffer[tail & MASK].get()).assume_init_read()
        };

        // Release: makes slot available to producer again
        self.rb.tail.store(tail.wrapping_add(1), Ordering::Release);
        Some(value)
    }
}

fn main() {
    let rb = RingBuffer::<u64>::new();
    let producer = Producer { rb: rb.clone() };
    let consumer = Consumer { rb };

    let t = std::thread::spawn(move || {
        for i in 0..32u64 {
            while !producer.push(i) { std::hint::spin_loop(); }
        }
    });

    let mut received = Vec::new();
    while received.len() < 32 {
        if let Some(v) = consumer.pop() {
            received.push(v);
        } else {
            std::hint::spin_loop();
        }
    }

    t.join().unwrap();
    println!("{:?}", &received[..8]);
    assert_eq!(received, (0..32).collect::<Vec<_>>());
}
```

output:
<think>
Tower's Service trait is the foundation of async middleware in Rust (used by Axum, Tonic, Hyper). Each service is: poll_ready + call. Middleware wraps an inner service and adds behaviour.

I'll show a logging middleware and a timeout middleware stacked together, with the tower::ServiceBuilder pattern.
</think>

```rust
use std::future::Future;
use std::pin::Pin;
use std::task::{Context, Poll};
use std::time::Instant;
use tower::{Service, ServiceBuilder, ServiceExt};
use tower::util::BoxCloneService;

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Request {
    method: String,
    path: String,
    body: Vec<u8>,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct Response {
    status: u16,
    body: Vec<u8>,
}

// ---- Inner "application" service ----
#[derive(Clone)]
struct AppService;

impl Service<Request> for AppService {
    type Response = Response;
    type Error = String;
    type Future = Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output = Result<Response, String>> + Send>>;

    fn poll_ready(&mut self, _cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Result<(), Self::Error>> {
        Poll::Ready(Ok(()))
    }

    fn call(&mut self, req: Request) -> Self::Future {
        Box::pin(async move {
            // Simulate async work
            tokio::time::sleep(tokio::time::Duration::from_millis(10)).await;
            Ok(Response {
                status: 200,
                body: format!("Hello from {}", req.path).into_bytes(),
            })
        })
    }
}

// ---- Logging middleware ----
#[derive(Clone)]
struct LoggingService<S> {
    inner: S,
}

impl<S> Service<Request> for LoggingService<S>
where
    S: Service<Request, Response = Response, Error = String> + Send + Clone + 'static,
    S::Future: Send,
{
    type Response = Response;
    type Error = String;
    type Future = Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output = Result<Response, String>> + Send>>;

    fn poll_ready(&mut self, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Result<(), Self::Error>> {
        self.inner.poll_ready(cx)
    }

    fn call(&mut self, req: Request) -> Self::Future {
        let start = Instant::now();
        let path = req.path.clone();
        let method = req.method.clone();
        let mut inner = self.inner.clone();

        Box::pin(async move {
            let result = inner.call(req).await;
            let elapsed = start.elapsed();
            match &result {
                Ok(r) => println!("[{method}] {path} -> {} ({elapsed:?})", r.status),
                Err(e) => println!("[{method}] {path} -> ERROR: {e} ({elapsed:?})"),
            }
            result
        })
    }
}

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    let mut svc = LoggingService {
        inner: AppService,
    };

    let req = Request {
        method: "GET".into(),
        path: "/api/users".into(),
        body: vec![],
    };

    let resp = svc.ready().await.unwrap().call(req).await.unwrap();
    println!("Response body: {}", String::from_utf8_lossy(&resp.body));

    // With ServiceBuilder for stacking:
    // let svc = ServiceBuilder::new()
    //     .timeout(Duration::from_secs(5))  // tower::timeout::Timeout layer
    //     .map_err(|e: Box<dyn std::error::Error>| e.to_string())
    //     .layer_fn(|s| LoggingService { inner: s })
    //     .service(AppService);
}
```

output:
<think>
An object pool reuses expensive-to-create objects (DB connections, thread handles, large buffers). The key design:
1. Checkout: get an object from the pool (blocks if none available)
2. Return: automatically happens when the RAII guard is dropped
3. The pool holds objects in a channel — easy async wait when empty

I'll use an mpsc channel as the backing store so checkout blocks cleanly via recv().
</think>

```rust
use tokio::sync::mpsc;
use std::sync::Arc;
use std::ops::{Deref, DerefMut};

pub struct Pool<T> {
    tx: mpsc::Sender<T>,
    rx: tokio::sync::Mutex<mpsc::Receiver<T>>,
}

impl<T: Send + 'static> Pool<T> {
    pub fn new(objects: Vec<T>) -> Arc<Self> {
        let (tx, rx) = mpsc::channel(objects.len().max(1));
        let pool = Arc::new(Pool {
            tx,
            rx: tokio::sync::Mutex::new(rx),
        });
        // Pre-populate the pool
        for obj in objects {
            pool.tx.try_send(obj).expect("pool channel should have capacity");
        }
        pool
    }

    pub async fn checkout(self: &Arc<Self>) -> Checkout<T> {
        let obj = self.rx.lock().await
            .recv().await
            .expect("pool was dropped");
        Checkout {
            obj: Some(obj),
            pool: self.tx.clone(),
        }
    }

    pub fn try_checkout(self: &Arc<Self>) -> Option<T> {
        self.tx.try_send(todo!()).err(); // placeholder
        None
    }
}

/// RAII guard: returns the object to the pool when dropped
pub struct Checkout<T> {
    obj: Option<T>,
    pool: mpsc::Sender<T>,
}

impl<T> Deref for Checkout<T> {
    type Target = T;
    fn deref(&self) -> &T { self.obj.as_ref().unwrap() }
}

impl<T> DerefMut for Checkout<T> {
    fn deref_mut(&mut self) -> &mut T { self.obj.as_mut().unwrap() }
}

impl<T: Send + 'static> Drop for Checkout<T> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if let Some(obj) = self.obj.take() {
            // Return to pool — if the pool is dropped, send fails silently
            let _ = self.pool.try_send(obj);
        }
    }
}

// --- Demo ---
struct FakeDbConn { id: u32 }

impl FakeDbConn {
    async fn query(&self, sql: &str) -> String {
        format!("[conn-{}] result of `{sql}`", self.id)
    }
}

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    let pool = Pool::new(vec![
        FakeDbConn { id: 1 },
        FakeDbConn { id: 2 },
        FakeDbConn { id: 3 },
    ]);

    let mut tasks = Vec::new();
    for i in 0..6 {
        let p = pool.clone();
        tasks.push(tokio::spawn(async move {
            let conn = p.checkout().await;
            let result = conn.query(&format!("SELECT {i}")).await;
            println!("{result}");
            // conn dropped here -> returned to pool
        }));
    }

    for t in tasks { t.await.unwrap(); }
}
```

**How it works:** The `mpsc::Receiver` is behind a `Mutex` so only one task can wait for an available object at a time. When a `Checkout` is dropped, `try_send` returns the object, which wakes the next waiter.
